title: Elasticities (PED, YED, XED, PES)
summary:
## What elasticity measures

**Elasticity** measures how *responsive* one variable is to a change in another. All elasticities are calculated as:

> **% change in the variable affected ÷ % change in the variable causing it**

There are four you must know: **PED, YED, XED** (demand-side) and **PES** (supply-side).

## 1. Price elasticity of demand (PED)

**PED = % change in quantity demanded ÷ % change in price.** It is normally **negative** (law of demand); we usually quote the size.

| Value | Term | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Perfectly inelastic | Qd doesn't change |
| 0 to −1 | Inelastic | Qd changes proportionately less than price |
| −1 | Unit elastic | Qd changes in exact proportion |
| −1 to ∞ | Elastic | Qd changes proportionately more than price |
| ∞ | Perfectly elastic | Any price rise → Qd falls to zero |

**Determinants of PED — mnemonic SPLAT:**
- **S**ubstitutes available (more → more elastic)
- **P**roportion of income spent (larger → more elastic)
- **L**uxury or necessity (luxuries elastic; necessities inelastic)
- **A**ddictiveness/habit (addictive → inelastic)
- **T**ime period (longer → more elastic)

**PED and total revenue (TR = P × Q):**
- Demand **inelastic** → raise price to raise TR.
- Demand **elastic** → lower price to raise TR.
- **Unit elastic** → TR is maximised/unchanged.
This is vital for firms' pricing and for tax policy.

## 2. Income elasticity of demand (YED)

**YED = % change in Qd ÷ % change in income.**
- **Normal good:** YED positive. (necessity: 0–1; luxury: >1)
- **Inferior good:** YED negative (demand falls as income rises).
Useful for firms planning for booms/recessions and for understanding structural change.

## 3. Cross elasticity of demand (XED)

**XED = % change in Qd of good A ÷ % change in price of good B.**
- **Substitutes:** XED **positive** (price of one up → demand for other up).
- **Complements:** XED **negative** (price of one up → demand for other down).
- **Unrelated:** XED ≈ 0.
The larger the magnitude, the closer the relationship.

## 4. Price elasticity of supply (PES)

**PES = % change in quantity supplied ÷ % change in price**; normally **positive**.

**Determinants:** spare capacity, availability of stocks, ease/speed of switching production, mobility of factors, and **time** — supply is more **elastic in the long run**. Agricultural goods are inelastic in the short run (long growing seasons).

## Why it matters

Elasticities turn demand & supply into a *quantitative, predictive* tool — they drive firm pricing, the incidence of taxes and subsidies, the effect of exchange-rate changes on trade, and the success of price controls. Expect calculation and application in every paper.

keyTerms.definition: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in the good's own price.
PED between 0 and −1: quantity changes proportionately less than price.
PED beyond −1: quantity changes proportionately more than price.
The responsiveness of demand to a change in consumer income.
A good with positive YED — demand rises as income rises.
A good with negative YED — demand falls as income rises.
The responsiveness of demand for one good to a price change of another.
The responsiveness of quantity supplied to a change in price.
Price × quantity sold; its response to a price change depends on PED.

diagrams: **PED extremes:** draw a vertical demand curve (perfectly inelastic) and a horizontal one (perfectly elastic); show a steep (inelastic) vs shallow (elastic) curve.
**PED & total revenue:** on a linear demand curve, shade the TR rectangle and show how it changes when price falls in the elastic vs inelastic range.
**PES:** draw a steep (inelastic) and a shallow (elastic) supply curve; a supply line through the origin is unit elastic.
tips: Show the **formula and working** in calculations and keep the **sign** (negative PED/XED tells you the relationship — don't just drop it).
Link **PED to total revenue**: inelastic → raise price; elastic → cut price. A classic application question.
Use **SPLAT** to generate determinants of PED quickly and fully.
Remember supply (and demand) are **more elastic in the long run** — always consider the time period.
Apply elasticity to policy: governments tax **inelastic** demerit goods (tobacco) to raise revenue with little fall in quantity — a strong evaluation link.
